headline: Iran reopens Strait of Hormuz after Lebanon ceasefire takes effect, but US maintains naval blockade on Iranian ports pending full peace deal

sections.label: Situation Overview
sections.title: Fragile Progress as Strait Opens Temporarily
paragraphs: Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz 'completely open' to commercial vessels following the start of a 10-day Israel-Lebanon ceasefire that began at 5pm Thursday. President Trump welcomed Iran's announcement, posting that the strait is 'ready for full passage', though the US naval blockade of Iranian ports will remain 'in full force' until a complete peace deal is reached.

Celebratory gunfire erupted across Beirut after midnight as Lebanese residents welcomed the ceasefire, with displaced families beginning to move toward southern Lebanon despite official warnings. Meanwhile, leaders from 40 countries joined a UK-France virtual summit to discuss establishing an international mission to reopen shipping routes through the strait.
key_facts: Iran reopened Strait of Hormuz for commercial vessels during 10-day Lebanon ceasefire
US maintains naval blockade on Iranian ports until full peace agreement
40-nation summit convened to discuss international shipping security mission
Lebanon ceasefire began Thursday 5pm, first pause in fighting in six weeks
sections.australia_relevance: Australia is expected to participate in the international shipping security mission being coordinated by Britain and France.

sections.label: Background
sections.title: 48-Day War Reaches Critical Juncture
paragraphs: The conflict began February 28 when the US and Israel launched coordinated strikes on Iran, targeting nuclear facilities and destroying IRGC buildings in Tehran. Iran retaliated by closing the Strait of Hormuz and launching missiles at US bases across the Middle East, disrupting 20% of global oil supplies.

Iran's forensic chief reports over 3,300 people killed since strikes began, while Israeli estimates suggest 3,000-4,000 Iranian military personnel have died. Pakistan mediated a two-week ceasefire on April 8, though both sides have violated the agreement since.
key_facts: War began February 28 with US-Israeli strikes on Iranian facilities
Iran closed Strait of Hormuz, disrupting 20% of global oil trade
Over 3,300 Iranian civilians killed, thousands of military casualties
Pakistan mediating ceasefire talks between US and Iran
sections.australia_relevance: Australia imports 90% of its refined fuel, making it highly vulnerable to Strait of Hormuz disruptions.

sections.label: Diplomacy
sections.title: Multiple Tracks Seek Lasting Resolution
paragraphs: Trump hinted at imminent developments, telling a Las Vegas crowd to 'watch what happens over the next week or so' and claiming Iran is now more willing to negotiate than before. In-person US-Iran talks could resume as early as this week, with Pakistani officials shuttling between capitals.

The 40-nation summit will establish a 'strictly defensive' international mission to secure shipping once conditions allow, focusing on mine clearance and commercial vessel safety. Pakistani Prime Minister Sharif has been conducting 'fuel diplomacy' visits to Saudi Arabia and Qatar, with Trump expressing optimism about achieving 'amazing' results in upcoming negotiations.
key_facts: 40 countries coordinating international shipping security mission
Pakistan mediating between US and Iran, with talks possibly resuming this week
Trump suggests major developments possible within days
Iran demands 10-point plan including sanctions relief and US withdrawal
sections.australia_relevance: Australia is participating in diplomatic efforts and the proposed international shipping security mission.

sections.label: Economy & Energy
sections.title: Global Markets React to Temporary Opening
paragraphs: Oil prices dropped 12% and stocks surged on news of the Strait reopening, though shipping companies say outstanding questions remain. Australian analysts predict petrol prices could jump 40 cents per litre if disruptions continue, adding $24 to fill a 60-litre tank.

Australian Finance Minister Jim Chalmers warned the country faces a 'really dangerous time' with higher inflation and slower growth expected, saying 'Australians didn't choose this war but are paying a hefty price'. The government has halved federal fuel taxes for three months and states are offering free public transport to reduce demand.
key_facts: Oil prices fell 12% on strait reopening news, but remain volatile
Australian petrol could rise 40 cents per litre if disruptions persist
Australia has cut fuel taxes in half and offered free public transport
IMF warns of global recession risk if conflict continues
sections.australia_relevance: Australia has only 26-36 days of fuel reserves and faces significant economic impacts from prolonged disruptions.

sections.label: What to Watch
sections.title: Critical Week Ahead for Peace Efforts
paragraphs: Iran warns of 'necessary measures' if the US naval blockade continues, with officials citing 'contradictory positions' from Washington despite the strait reopening. Israeli PM Netanyahu vowed to continue 'dismantling' Hezbollah despite the ceasefire, saying the job isn't finished.

Despite Iran's announcement, shipping through the Strait remains far below normal levels with only small numbers of vessels moving through the route. The prospects for lasting settlement remain unclear, with the IEA assuming regular deliveries might resume by mid-year but warning this could prove too optimistic.
key_facts: Lebanon ceasefire lasts only 10 days, fragile and contested
Shipping levels remain far below normal despite strait reopening
Iran threatens retaliation if US blockade continues
Trump hinting at major developments within next week
sections.australia_relevance: Australia's fuel security depends on sustained reopening of shipping lanes and resolution of the broader conflict.
